w:r.w:t: Faculty of Computers and Artificial Intelligence
Submitted in Partial Fulfillment of the Requirements for the Degree of
Bachelor of Computers and Artificial Intelligence
We hereby declare that this graduation project entitled "Car Marketplace Platform" is our own original work and has not been submitted previously for any degree or qualification at any institution.
All sources of information, references, and materials used from external works have been properly acknowledged and cited within this report.
We understand that any form of plagiarism or academic misconduct may result in disciplinary action according to university regulations.

The rapid growth of digital marketplaces has transformed the way vehicles are bought and sold, creating a demand for platforms that provide secure transactions, intelligent pricing assistance, and seamless communication between users. However, many existing car marketplace solutions lack advanced pricing support, real-time interaction capabilities, and integrated administrative management features. This project presents the design and development of an AI-enhanced Car Marketplace platform that combines modern web technologies with machine learning techniques to improve the overall experience for both car buyers and sellers.
The system was developed using a modern multi-tier architecture. The frontend was implemented using
, providing a responsive and user-friendly interface, while the backend was built using
following Clean Architecture principles to ensure scalability, maintainability, and separation of concerns.
was used for persistent data storage, and
was integrated to enable real-time communication between users. Authentication and authorization were implemented using
, providing secure user sessions and role-based access control for both regular users and administrators.

A key feature of the platform is the integration of an Artificial Intelligence module for vehicle price estimation. The AI service utilizes a machine learning model trained on an Egyptian-market vehicle dataset containing attributes such as brand, model, manufacturing year, mileage, fuel type, transmission type, vehicle condition, and location. Based on these attributes, the model generates an estimated market price, confidence score, price range, and a classification indicating whether the seller’s proposed price is low, normal, or high compared to expected market values. To ensure reliability, a fallback estimation mechanism was implemented to maintain service availability when the machine learning service is temporarily unavailable.

In addition to intelligent pricing, the platform supports vehicle listing management, image uploads, advanced search and filtering, user profile management, password recovery and reset functionality, administrative dashboards, and a real-time messaging system. The messaging subsystem includes delivery status tracking, read receipts, typing indicators, online
presence detection, and last-seen functionality, providing a communication experience similar to modern messaging applications.

The results demonstrate that integrating machine learning techniques with a vehicle marketplace platform can improve pricing transparency, support informed decision-making, and enhance user trust. The developed system provides a scalable foundation for future improvements, including the integration of real-world automotive datasets, recommendation systems, and large-scale production deployment.
Car Marketplace, Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Price Estimation, Next.js, ASP.NET Core, SignalR, Real-Time Messaging, JWT Authentication, Vehicle Pricing.

First and foremost, we would like to express our sincere gratitude to our supervisor for his valuable guidance, continuous support, and constructive feedback throughout the development of this project. His expertise, encouragement, and insightful suggestions played a significant role in helping us complete this work successfully.

We would also like to thank the faculty members and teaching staff of the Faculty of Computers and Artificial Intelligence for providing us with the knowledge and skills that formed the foundation of this project. Their dedication and support throughout our academic journey have been invaluable.

Special thanks are extended to our colleagues and friends who contributed through discussions, feedback, and encouragement during the various stages of the project. Their support helped us overcome many challenges and improve the quality of our work.

We are deeply grateful to our families for their endless patience, understanding, and unwavering support throughout our studies. Their encouragement and belief in our abilities have been a constant source of motivation.

Finally, we would like to thank everyone who contributed directly or indirectly to the completion of this project. This achievement would not have been possible without their support and encouragement.

Buying and selling used cars online requires users to make decisions based on incomplete, inconsistent, and often subjective information. Sellers may not know how to price their vehicles fairly, especially when vehicle value depends on many attributes such as brand, model, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission type, condition, and location. As a result, listings may be overpriced, reducing buyer interest, or underpriced, causing financial loss to the seller.

Traditional classified platforms mainly provide listing publication and basic browsing. They often lack intelligent pricing guidance during the listing process and do not help users evaluate whether a posted price is low, normal, or high compared with expected market value. This creates difficulty for buyers who lack market expertise and for sellers who need transparent support before publishing a listing.

Communication is another problem. Buyer-seller interaction is often fragmented across external messaging applications or delayed contact methods. This weakens negotiation efficiency and makes it harder for users to track conversations, message delivery, online availability, and response status inside the marketplace itself.

Therefore, the problem addressed by this project is the need for an integrated car marketplace platform that combines secure listing management, intelligent AI-assisted price estimation, real-time buyer-seller communication, and administrative control in one coherent system.

The used-car market is highly sensitive to price transparency. A small pricing error may affect buyer trust, seller profit, and listing visibility. For many users, estimating the value of a used vehicle requires knowledge of market trends, model depreciation, mileage impact, and vehicle condition. The proposed system is motivated by the need to reduce this uncertainty by embedding AI-based price estimation directly into the car listing workflow.
The significance of the project is reflected in its value to the main stakeholders:
Receive an estimated market price range and price classification before publishing a listing, reducing the risk of unrealistic pricing.
View clearer listing information and communicate directly with sellers through real-time messaging.
Manage users and listings through an administrative panel, supporting moderation and platform control.
Benefit from a Clean Architecture backend, separated frontend, database layer, and ML service.

Real-time communication is also significant because buying a used car often requires immediate questions about availability, vehicle details, and negotiation. By using SignalR, the platform supports real-time message delivery, seen status, online/offline presence, and last-seen tracking. This makes the communication process more transparent and more suitable for marketplace interaction.

AI-assisted pricing is significant because it connects machine-learning predictions with practical marketplace decisions. The system does not only return an estimated price; it also classifies the user-entered price as
, helping sellers understand how their chosen price compares with the estimated value.
Existing online car marketplaces commonly focus on publishing and browsing listings. However, several gaps remain:
Many platforms provide car listings but limited intelligent pricing guidance.
Price estimation is often separated from the listing process rather than integrated into the seller workflow.
Users are rarely informed whether their entered price is low, normal, or high compared with an estimated market value.
Marketplace communication is often disconnected from the platform or lacks real-time delivery, seen, and presence feedback.
ML predictions are often treated as isolated outputs rather than connected to functional marketplace decisions such as listing creation and price evaluation.
This project addresses these gaps by integrating AI price estimation, price classification, real-time chat, listing management, and administration into a single platform.
RQ1: To what extent can a machine-learning model estimate used-car prices using vehicle attributes such as brand, model, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission type, condition, and location?
RQ2: Can AI-assisted price estimation reduce the likelihood of unrealistic listing prices by classifying user-entered prices as low, normal, or high?
RQ3: Can integrating real-time messaging improve buyer-seller interaction efficiency through instant messages, delivered status, seen status, and online/offline presence?
RQ4: Can a Clean Architecture ASP.NET Core backend support secure authentication, listing management, chat, administration, and AI integration in a maintainable structure?
RQ5: How reliable is the AI pricing workflow when the Python ML service is unavailable, and can fallback estimation preserve basic platform functionality?
The objectives of this project are:
Develop a secure AI-enhanced car marketplace platform.
Implement user registration, login, logout, password reset, password change, and profile management.
Provide car listing creation, editing, deletion, image upload, browsing, search, filtering, and pagination.
Integrate AI-based car price estimation into the listing workflow.
based on the relationship between user price and estimated price.
Enable real-time user-to-user messaging using SignalR.
Support delivered and seen message status, online/offline presence, and last-seen tracking.
Provide dashboard statistics for users and administrative management for users and listings.
Store application data using SQL Server and EF Core.
Maintain modularity through a Next.js frontend, ASP.NET Core backend, and Python Flask ML service.

The proposed solution is an AI-enhanced car marketplace platform built using a multi-layer architecture. The frontend is implemented using Next.js and provides the user interface for authentication, marketplace browsing, listing management, dashboards, chat, profile management, and administration.

The backend is implemented using ASP.NET Core with Clean Architecture. It separates API controllers, application services, domain entities, infrastructure repositories, and database access. SQL Server with EF Core is used to store users, cars, car images, messages, refresh tokens, password reset tokens, and dashboard-related data.

Authentication is handled using JWT access tokens and refresh tokens. The backend validates access tokens, stores refresh tokens securely as hashes, supports token rotation, and revokes sessions after logout, password reset, password change, or user deletion.

Real-time communication is implemented using SignalR. Users can send messages, receive messages instantly, view delivered and seen status, and observe online/offline presence and last-seen information.

The AI module is implemented as a Python Flask ML service. The ASP.NET Core backend calls the ML service for price prediction and wraps the result into a marketplace-friendly response containing estimated price, minimum price, maximum price, confidence score, insights, and price status classification. If the ML service is unavailable, the backend uses fallback rule-based estimation to keep the platform functional.
User → Listing Form → AI Price Estimation → Price Classification → Marketplace Listing → Buyer-Seller Messaging → Purchase Decision Support

The scope of the project includes car listing management, image uploads, AI-assisted price estimation, price classification, real-time chat, user dashboards, profile management, authentication, and admin management of users and listings.
The project does not perform physical vehicle inspection. Therefore, the AI price estimate cannot verify actual mechanical condition, accident history, legal status, or hidden defects.
The project does not guarantee the final sale price of a vehicle. The AI estimate is intended as decision support, not as an official valuation or binding market price.

The ML module currently uses synthetic Egypt-market used-car data during the graduation-project stage. Although the dataset is designed to represent realistic relationships between vehicle attributes and price, real marketplace data would be required for stronger production-level accuracy.
The system does not integrate directly with external automotive marketplaces, dealer systems, payment gateways, vehicle registration authorities, or insurance services.
The platform supports communication and decision-making but does not implement a complete purchase transaction, online payment, ownership transfer, or vehicle delivery workflow.

Presence tracking currently uses an in-memory SignalR connection tracker, which is suitable for a single-server deployment. A distributed presence store or SignalR backplane would be required for multi-server production deployment.
Chapter 1 introduces the problem, motivation, research gaps, research questions, project objectives, proposed solution, scope, and limitations.

Chapter 2 presents the literature review and background, covering online marketplaces, used-car price estimation, machine learning concepts, authentication, real-time communication, and related technologies.
Chapter 3 discusses analysis and requirements engineering, including stakeholder analysis, functional requirements, non-functional requirements, use cases, UML diagrams, and data requirements.
Chapter 4 presents the system design and architecture, including frontend design, backend Clean Architecture, database design, API structure, SignalR design, and ML service integration.
Chapter 5 explains implementation and testing, covering the developed modules, authentication flow, listing features, chat implementation, AI price estimation, admin features, and test cases.
Chapter 6 presents results, evaluation, conclusions, limitations, and future work, including ML evaluation, system evaluation, and recommended improvements for production deployment.

Online marketplaces are digital environments that connect buyers and sellers and reduce the need for traditional physical intermediaries. Early research on electronic marketplaces explains that internet-based market systems can reduce search costs, widen access to product information, and improve transaction efficiency when information is structured and searchable (Bakos, 1998). Unlike conventional e-commerce platforms, where products are often standardized and sold by a central business, peer-to-peer and classified marketplaces depend heavily on user-generated content. This makes marketplace quality strongly dependent on listing accuracy, seller reliability, buyer trust, communication quality, and price transparency.

Used-car marketplaces are more complex than many ordinary e-commerce domains because cars are heterogeneous goods. Two vehicles with the same brand and model may differ significantly in market value due to manufacturing year, mileage, transmission, fuel type, condition, location, and maintenance history. This is closely related to the classic “market for lemons” problem, where information asymmetry between sellers and buyers can reduce trust and distort market prices (Akerlof, 1970). In used-car trading, sellers often know more about the actual condition of the car, while buyers must make decisions based on observable listing data and communication with the seller.

This information asymmetry directly affects buyer-seller trust. Research on online marketplaces shows that institutional mechanisms and platform-supported trust features are important for increasing buyer confidence in seller communities (Pavlou & Gefen, 2004). In used-car platforms, trust is not only related to seller identity; it is also related to whether the listed price appears realistic. A buyer may distrust a listing if the price seems too high, while a seller may lose value if the price is too low.

Used-car pricing is therefore a central domain challenge. Manual pricing requires market expertise, comparison with similar cars, and knowledge of how vehicle attributes affect value. Pudaruth (2014) studied the use of supervised machine-learning techniques for used-car price prediction and showed that even a seemingly simple vehicle pricing problem can be difficult to solve accurately due to the number of interacting factors. This supports the need for intelligent pricing support inside car marketplace systems.

Real-time communication is also important in this domain. A buyer often needs to ask whether the vehicle is still available, whether the price is negotiable, or whether additional details can be provided. If communication is external to the platform, the marketplace loses visibility over interaction quality. Real-time systems based on WebSocket communication support full-duplex communication between client and server, enabling instant updates instead of repeated request polling (Fette & Melnikov, 2011). For a marketplace, this enables direct chat, online/offline status, delivered receipts, seen receipts, and last-seen tracking.

Pricing transparency and real-time interaction together improve marketplace trust. An intelligent used-car marketplace should therefore not only publish listings but also support sellers in selecting reasonable prices and support buyers in communicating efficiently with sellers. This thesis builds on that direction by integrating AI price estimation, price classification, listing management, and real-time chat into one platform.

Modern marketplace solutions may be divided into traditional listing-centric platforms and more intelligent decision-support platforms. Traditional classified platforms, such as dubbizle(OLX Previously)-style systems, focus on allowing users to publish listings, browse categories, search products, and contact sellers. Their main advantage is simplicity. They are flexible and can support many product categories. However, their limitation is that they usually depend on the seller to determine price manually and on the buyer to judge whether that price is fair.

Facebook Marketplace style platforms combine listings with social identity. This may increase perceived trust because users can sometimes view seller profiles, mutual connections, or social activity. However, these platforms remain general-purpose. They are not designed specifically around automotive pricing attributes such as year, mileage, fuel type, condition, and transmission. Their communication layer may support messaging, but their decision-support features for vehicle valuation are limited.

Automotive-specific marketplaces improve domain structure by supporting car-related filters such as brand, model, year, mileage, price range, fuel type, and transmission. Such systems are closer to the needs of used-car buyers and sellers. However, many vehicle marketplaces still separate valuation tools from listing creation. The user may need to estimate price manually or consult a separate calculator. This separation creates a workflow gap between pricing intelligence and listing publication.

AI-assisted decision-support systems address this limitation by embedding prediction and evaluation into the user workflow. In an intelligent marketplace, AI can estimate expected value, detect abnormal prices, recommend comparable listings, or classify whether a user-entered price is reasonable. This direction aligns with machine-learning research in vehicle price prediction, where supervised models are trained to estimate prices from historical attributes (Pudaruth, 2014).

Real-time communication systems are another important part of modern marketplaces. WebSocket-based communication allows the server to push updates to the client immediately, rather than requiring the client to repeatedly request updates (Fette & Melnikov, 2011). In this project, SignalR is used as the real-time communication layer. It supports events such as message received, message delivered, message seen, typing, stop typing, online, offline, and last-seen updates.
AI-assisted price estimation and price classification
Real-time chat, delivery status, seen status, presence
Transparent price guidance and interaction tracking

The proposed AI-Enhanced Car Marketplace Platform belongs to the second category. It does not treat the platform as only a listing board. Instead, it integrates AI price estimation into the listing workflow and supports real-time communication between buyers and sellers.

Traditional vehicle pricing approaches include rule-based systems, expert systems, and statistical regression models. Rule-based systems apply predefined rules, such as reducing price by a fixed percentage for every year of vehicle age or applying a penalty when mileage exceeds a certain threshold. These systems are easy to understand and simple to implement, but they cannot learn from changing market behavior unless the rules are manually updated.

Expert systems improve rule-based approaches by encoding knowledge from domain specialists. For example, an expert system may define that premium brands depreciate differently from budget brands, automatic transmission may increase price, or hybrid fuel type may have a different market effect than petrol. Expert systems are explainable, but they depend heavily on the quality of human-defined rules.

Linear Regression is one of the most common statistical methods for estimating continuous values. It models the relationship between one or more independent variables and a dependent variable. In car pricing, the dependent variable is price, while independent variables may include year, mileage, brand, and condition. Standard regression analysis is widely used because it is mathematically interpretable and computationally efficient (Draper & Smith, 1998).
The basic linear regression model can be written as:
Price = β0 + β1X1 + β2X2 + ... + βnXn + ε

Multiple Regression extends this concept by using several predictors together. For example, a car price model may combine year, mileage, fuel type, transmission, and condition in one equation. The benefit of this approach is interpretability: coefficients can show whether a feature increases or decreases price. However, used-car pricing is not always linear. The impact of mileage may be stronger after a threshold, depreciation may differ by brand, and condition may interact with year and mileage.

Classification and Regression Trees introduced a more flexible family of models that can split data into regions based on feature values (Breiman et al., 1984). For pricing, a tree may split first by year, then mileage, then brand, and finally produce a predicted price at the leaf node. This approach is easier to interpret than many complex models and can represent non-linear rules. However, a single tree may overfit the training data and produce unstable predictions.

In the proposed system, rule-based pricing remains useful as a fallback mechanism when the ML service is unavailable. However, the main intelligent pricing direction is based on machine learning because used-car pricing depends on mixed numerical and categorical variables with non-linear relationships.

Machine learning approaches are suitable for used-car price estimation because they learn patterns directly from data. A supervised learning model is trained on records where input features describe the vehicle and the target output is the known price. After training, the model estimates the price of a new vehicle based on its attributes.
The common features used in vehicle pricing include:
Captures manufacturer reputation and market segment
Represents model-specific demand and base value
Reflects market preference and vehicle category

Decision Trees can model non-linear relationships by recursively splitting the dataset. For example, the model may learn that a newer car with low mileage has a different price pattern than an older car with high mileage. Trees are useful because their decisions can be inspected, but individual trees often have high variance.

Random Forest improves this by training many trees and averaging their outputs. Breiman (2001) introduced Random Forests as an ensemble method that combines multiple decision trees to improve prediction stability and accuracy. The simplified prediction formula is:
is the number of trees and
is the prediction of the tree

Random Forest is particularly useful for tabular datasets because it handles non-linear effects, feature interactions, and mixed variable types. It can also produce feature-importance estimates, which help determine whether year, mileage, brand, or condition contributes most to the prediction.

Gradient Boosting takes a different approach. Instead of training independent trees, it trains models sequentially so that each new model attempts to correct the errors of previous models. Friedman (2001) formalized gradient boosting as a method for function approximation through additive models. The prediction can be understood conceptually as:
Prediction = Initial estimate + Correction1 + Correction2 + ... + CorrectionN

XGBoost is an optimized gradient-boosting system designed for scalability and performance on structured data. Chen and Guestrin (2016) introduced XGBoost as a scalable tree boosting system that includes regularization and efficient computation. Its objective function combines prediction error with a penalty for model complexity:

This is important for used-car pricing because the model must capture complex relationships without overfitting. For example, the effect of mileage may depend on vehicle age, the effect of fuel type may depend on model category, and premium brands may behave differently from budget brands.

Handling mixed categorical and numerical data is a key technical requirement. Numerical values such as year and mileage may be scaled or transformed. Categorical features such as brand, fuel type, transmission, and condition may be encoded using one-hot encoding or other categorical representations. Applied predictive modeling literature emphasizes that preprocessing and feature preparation are central to model performance, especially for structured datasets (Kuhn & Johnson, 2013).
Machine learning approaches provide several advantages:
They learn from data rather than fixed rules.
They can be retrained when market data changes.

However, they also have limitations. Their accuracy depends on training data quality and whether the data represents the real market. If synthetic data is used, predictions should be treated as decision support rather than guaranteed market values. This limitation is directly relevant to the project because the ML service currently uses a synthetic Egypt-market dataset during the graduation-project stage.

Deep learning uses artificial neural networks to learn complex functions from data. A neural network consists of layers of connected nodes, where each node applies a weighted transformation followed by an activation function. Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville (2016) describe deep learning as representation learning through multiple layers, allowing models to learn increasingly abstract patterns.
A simple neural computation may be represented as:

Artificial Neural Networks and Deep Neural Networks can be applied to used-car pricing. Categorical variables such as brand or model may be represented using embeddings, while numeric variables such as mileage and year may be normalized and passed into dense layers. These methods can learn complex interactions, but they usually require larger datasets and careful regularization.

Transformer-based models are a more recent deep-learning family. Vaswani et al. (2017) introduced the Transformer architecture, which relies on self-attention rather than recurrent processing. The attention mechanism allows the model to assign importance to different parts of the input. The scaled dot-product attention formula is:

Transformers are highly effective for sequential and language-based tasks, and transformer-inspired models have also been adapted to tabular learning. However, they may be excessive for relatively structured used-car pricing datasets. Vehicle price estimation usually uses tabular attributes such as brand, model, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission, condition, and location. Tree-based models such as Random Forest and XGBoost often perform strongly on such data while requiring less training data and fewer computational resources.
Often unnecessary for small or medium tabular datasets
Less suitable for unstructured text/image-heavy inputs
For this project, tree-based machine learning is more appropriate than deep learning because the pricing task is based mainly on structured vehicle attributes and the dataset size is moderate.

Feature engineering transforms raw data into representations that improve model learning. In structured prediction tasks, the quality of engineered features may significantly affect model performance (Kuhn & Johnson, 2013). For used-car pricing, raw listing data may include missing values, inconsistent text, invalid years, unrealistic mileage, or abnormal prices.

Data cleaning includes removing invalid rows, filling or rejecting missing values, and correcting inconsistent formats. For example, year must not be greater than the current year, mileage must not be negative, and price must be positive. Without cleaning, the model may learn from noise rather than meaningful pricing relationships.

Normalization is necessary because the same value may appear in different forms. For example, “petrol” and “gasoline” may refer to the same fuel type. Similarly, “semi automatic,” “semi-automatic,” and “SemiAutomatic” should be represented consistently. If these values are not normalized, the model treats them as separate categories, which weakens learning.

Outlier removal or clipping is also important. Very high prices may represent luxury cars, incorrect data entry, or abnormal listings. Very low prices may represent damaged vehicles, scams, or missing digits. Proper outlier handling prevents extreme values from dominating the learning process.
Derived features are especially valuable in car pricing:
Whether mileage is normal or high for age
Approximate value reduction due to age

The proposed project’s ML pipeline uses derived features such as car age, mileage per year, condition score, and brand tier. These features are more informative than raw values alone. For example, mileage of 80,000 km has different meaning for a two-year-old car than for a ten-year-old car. Mileage per year therefore provides a more useful measure of usage intensity.

Feature engineering is also used after prediction. The project converts the numeric estimated price into a price-status label. If the seller-entered price is more than 15% below the estimate, it is classified as
. If it is more than 15% above the estimate, it is classified as
. This conversion makes the model output more usable for marketplace decision-making.

Explainable AI aims to make model outputs understandable to humans. This is especially important in domains where predictions influence user decisions. In used-car marketplaces, a seller is more likely to trust an estimated price if the system provides understandable reasons, such as mileage being high, year being recent, or the brand belonging to a premium segment.

Feature importance is one basic explainability method. It identifies which features generally influence model predictions. For example, a model may show that year, mileage, brand, and condition are the most important attributes in price estimation.

SHAP is a more advanced explainability method. Lundberg and Lee (2017) proposed SHAP as a unified approach to interpreting model predictions based on Shapley values. SHAP explains how much each feature contributes to a specific prediction. For a car pricing system, SHAP could show that low mileage increased the estimated price while an older manufacturing year reduced it.

Molnar (2022) emphasizes that interpretability is essential when model predictions need to be inspected, trusted, or communicated to users. This is relevant to marketplace pricing because price estimation affects seller confidence and buyer trust. A black-box estimate without explanation may be less useful than an estimate accompanied by confidence, range, and insights.
Avoids false precision from one number

The current project includes practical explainability through confidence score, estimated range, and insights. A future improvement would be to add SHAP-based explanations to show how each feature affected each individual estimate.

AI-enhanced marketplaces require both machine-learning capability and strong software engineering design. A prediction model alone does not create a useful system unless it is integrated into secure authentication, reliable APIs, real-time communication, persistent storage, frontend workflows, and administrative tools.

Clean Architecture separates business rules from frameworks, databases, and user-interface concerns. Martin (2017) argues that software architecture should protect core business logic from external implementation details. This is relevant to the project because listing management, authentication, messaging, and AI integration must remain maintainable as the system evolves.

Layered enterprise architecture is also important. Fowler (2002) describes patterns for organizing enterprise applications into layers such as presentation, domain logic, data access, and service boundaries. The proposed project follows this principle through a Next.js frontend, ASP.NET Core API layer, application services, domain entities, infrastructure repositories, and SQL Server persistence.

Microservices architecture separates system capabilities into independently deployable services. Newman (2015) explains that microservices can improve autonomy and independent deployment but also introduce distributed-system complexity. In the proposed project, the Python Flask ML service is separated from the ASP.NET Core backend. This allows the ML component to use Python libraries and model artifacts independently, while the backend remains responsible for marketplace logic.

REST APIs are based on architectural principles described by Fielding (2000), including statelessness, uniform interfaces, and resource-oriented communication. In the project, REST endpoints are suitable for operations such as registering users, creating car listings, retrieving cars, updating profiles, managing admin data, and requesting AI price estimation.

Real-time communication requires a different model. The WebSocket protocol supports persistent full-duplex communication between client and server (Fette & Melnikov, 2011). SignalR builds on this real-time communication model by enabling server-to-client events. In the marketplace context, this supports instant messages, message delivery, seen receipts, typing indicators, online presence, offline events, and last-seen tracking.

Authentication and session management are also central. JSON Web Token (JWT) is defined as a compact token format for transferring claims between parties (Jones et al., 2015). JWTs are suitable for stateless access-token authentication because the backend can validate token signature, issuer, audience, expiry, and claims. However, access tokens should usually be short-lived, which creates the need for refresh tokens.

OAuth 2.0 defines refresh tokens as credentials used to obtain new access tokens without requiring the user to authenticate again (Hardt, 2012). In secure systems, refresh tokens should be protected, rotated, revoked when necessary, and monitored for reuse. The proposed project follows this direction by storing refresh tokens as hashes, rotating them, revoking them on logout/password change/password reset/user deletion, and detecting reuse.

MLOps is the discipline of operating machine-learning models as maintainable production systems. Sculley et al. (2015) warned that ML systems can create hidden technical debt through data dependencies, configuration issues, undeclared consumers, and changing external environments. This is highly relevant to car pricing because market prices change over time, and the ML model must eventually be retrained and monitored.

Amershi et al. (2019) studied software engineering practices for machine-learning systems and showed that AI applications require workflows that combine data management, model development, deployment, monitoring, and software engineering. Kreuzberger, Kühl, and Hirschl (2023) further define MLOps as an approach that combines machine learning, DevOps, and data engineering for automated and reliable ML lifecycle management. Treveil et al. (2020) also emphasize model versioning, deployment pipelines, monitoring, retraining, and collaboration as core MLOps concerns.
Separates business rules from frameworks and infrastructure
Organizes frontend, API, application, domain, and persistence concerns
Separates Python ML service from ASP.NET Core backend
Enables real-time chat and presence events
Maintains sessions while supporting revocation and rotation
Supports model versioning, retraining, monitoring, and deployment

The proposed project therefore represents a cross-domain system. It combines marketplace design, AI price estimation, secure authentication, real-time communication, database persistence, and software architecture. This integration is essential because the value of the ML model depends on how effectively it is embedded into the marketplace workflow.

The reviewed literature shows that the project problem exists at the intersection of online marketplaces, used-car price prediction, real-time communication, and AI system engineering. Existing research and platforms address parts of the problem, but several gaps remain.
Gap 1: Many marketplaces lack integrated AI pricing during listing creation.

Traditional marketplaces reduce search costs and support digital exchange (Bakos, 1998), but they often remain listing-centric. Even automotive marketplaces may provide pricing tools separately from listing creation. This means sellers may still publish prices without real-time guidance inside the listing form.
Gap 2: Many pricing systems return a number but do not classify seller-entered prices.

Used-car price prediction studies commonly focus on estimating a numeric price and evaluating prediction accuracy (Pudaruth, 2014). However, a predicted price alone may not be easily actionable for a seller. The user needs to know whether their own entered price is low, normal, or high compared with the model estimate.
Gap 3: AI estimation and marketplace workflows are often disconnected.

Machine-learning models are often evaluated as standalone prediction systems. Software engineering research shows that integrating ML into real applications introduces additional lifecycle and maintenance concerns (Amershi et al., 2019; Sculley et al., 2015). Therefore, a research gap exists in connecting ML prediction with real marketplace functions such as listing creation, image upload, dashboard, and user communication.
Gap 4: Real-time communication is frequently external to the marketplace.

Many classified systems depend on phone calls, external chat applications, or delayed contact methods. This prevents the marketplace from tracking delivered status, seen status, online presence, and last-seen state. WebSocket-based systems make these features technically feasible (Fette & Melnikov, 2011), but they are not always integrated into car marketplace workflows.
Gap 5: Existing studies focus heavily on ML accuracy but less on full-system integration.

ML research often emphasizes model metrics, algorithm comparison, and prediction accuracy. These are important, but they do not fully evaluate whether the model improves the marketplace experience. A complete intelligent marketplace must combine ML with authentication, authorization, database consistency, frontend usability, real-time communication, and administrative management.
Gap 1: Limited AI pricing during listing creation
Sellers manually select price or use separate valuation tools
The listing form calls the AI price-estimation endpoint during listing creation
Numeric price alone may be difficult to interpret
The system classifies seller-entered price as
Gap 3: Disconnected ML and marketplace workflow
ML model may exist separately from operational marketplace features
The ASP.NET Core backend integrates ML output into the listing workflow
Buyer-seller communication may occur outside the platform
SignalR chat provides messages, delivered status, seen status, presence, and last seen
Gap 5: ML accuracy without full-system evaluation
Studies often focus on model metrics only
The project combines ML, secure APIs, SQL Server persistence, dashboard, admin tools, and real-time chat

This thesis addresses these gaps by implementing a complete AI-enhanced car marketplace platform. The contribution is not limited to training a price-prediction model. Instead, the project integrates AI estimation into the seller workflow, converts estimated price into actionable price classification, supports real-time buyer-seller interaction, and applies software engineering principles such as Clean Architecture, JWT authentication, refresh-token security, REST APIs, SignalR events, and ML service separation.
Browse available cars and decide whether to register
Browse marketplace, search/filter cars, view car details, register, login, forgot/reset password
Easy access, clear listing details, secure registration
Manage account, listings, messages, and dashboard
Login/logout, profile update, avatar upload, password change, dashboard, messages
Session security, privacy, stable access token/refresh flow
Create accurate listings and price them competitively
Add/edit/delete own listings, upload car images, request AI price estimate, apply suggested price, receive buyer messages
Image validation, fair AI estimate, listing ownership protection
Registered users or guests depending on action
Find suitable vehicles and communicate with sellers
Browse/filter/search cars, view owner details, message seller after login
Accurate filters, owner visibility, real-time chat reliability
View paginated users/listings, update user role, delete users, delete listings
Role-based authorization, auditability, avoiding accidental deletion
Provide usable marketplace, auth, dashboard, chat, and admin screens
Uses NextAuth, server actions, protected routes, SignalR client, forms, filters, pagination
API contract stability, error handling, route authorization
Provide secure REST and SignalR APIs
Controllers, services, repositories, EF Core, JWT, refresh tokens, uploads, ProblemDetails
Python Flask ML service plus .NET AI integration
Estimate car market price and classify user price
Dataset quality, response time, fallback behavior, model accuracy
Verify analysis, implementation, and documentation quality
Reviews requirements, UML, architecture, tests, ML pipeline, dataset assumptions
Traceability to code, non-generic documentation, correctness
The system shall allow a guest user to register using full name, email, and password.
The system shall authenticate users using email and password and return a JWT access token while storing the refresh token in an HTTP-only cookie.
The system shall allow logout by revoking the refresh token and clearing the refresh-token cookie.
The system shall support access-token refresh using a valid refresh token.
The system shall support forgot-password and reset-password flows using one-time reset tokens.
The system shall allow authenticated users to change their password and revoke active sessions after password change.
The system shall allow authenticated users to view/update profile data and upload an avatar.
The system shall allow guests and users to browse cars as a paginated list.
The system shall allow filtering cars by brand, model, price range, year range, and free-text search.
The system shall return available models for a selected brand as distinct sorted values from stored listings.
The system shall display car details with images, owner details, price, location, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission type, and description.
The system shall allow authenticated sellers to add a car listing with title, brand, model, description, price, location, year, mileage, fuel type, and transmission type.
The system shall allow sellers to upload car images when creating a listing.
The system shall allow only the listing owner or an Admin to edit/delete a car listing.
The system shall allow users to view their own listings through the My Cars page.
The system shall provide an AI estimated price, minimum price, maximum price, confidence score, and insights for a car.
The system shall provide an estimated price range and classify the user-entered price as
The system shall allow the seller to apply the AI suggested price in the listing form.
The system shall allow Admin users to view paginated users.
The system shall allow Admin users to update user roles and delete users.
The system shall allow Admin users to view and delete marketplace listings.
The system shall allow authenticated users to view conversation summaries and message history.
The system shall allow authenticated users to send messages to other users.
The system shall send real-time chat events using SignalR, including message received, message deleted, delivered, seen, read, typing, stop typing, online, and offline events.
The system shall track delivered and seen message status with timestamps.
The system shall track online/offline presence and last-seen timestamps.
The system shall provide a user dashboard containing total owned listings, total listing views, unread message count, and recent activity.
Listings exist or empty result is allowed
Add Car Listing with AI Estimate
Active listing is stored with owner and images
User owns listing or is Admin
Message is stored and real-time events are emitted
User role changes or user is deleted
Listing list is viewed or listing is deleted
Use Case Description: Add Car Listing With AI Price Estimate
Seller is authenticated. Required car fields are entered. Images are selected for the multipart listing endpoint.
Seller opens Add Car dialog. Seller enters brand, model, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission, location, and price. Frontend calls

. Backend validates input and calls AI service. AI service returns estimated price, min/max range, confidence score, insights, and optional price status. Seller optionally applies suggested price. Seller submits listing with images. Backend validates and stores images, creates the listing, assigns current user as owner, and returns created car.

If AI service is unavailable, backend uses rule-based fallback. If image type/size is invalid, creation is rejected. If user is unauthenticated, request returns unauthorized. If car data is invalid, validation returns bad request.
Listing is stored as active with views initialized to zero, owner ID assigned, and image URLs stored.
Use Case Description: Send Real-Time Message
Sender is authenticated. Receiver exists. Sender and receiver are different users.

User opens chat from car details or messages page. Frontend loads conversations and message history. User sends message through REST or SignalR hub. Backend uses authenticated user as sender, validates receiver, stores message, checks receiver online state, and emits
. If receiver is online, message is marked delivered and
is emitted. When conversation is opened/read, messages are marked seen and

If receiver is offline, message remains undelivered until receiver connects. If SignalR notification fails, the REST message operation still succeeds. If receiver does not exist or sender equals receiver, request is rejected.
Message is persisted with created time, delivery/seen state, and optional car reference. Conversation summaries and unread counts are updated.
Use Case Description: Admin Delete User
User is authenticated with Admin role. Target user exists.

Admin opens Admin Panel. Admin selects Users tab. Admin confirms delete. Backend calls Admin user delete endpoint. User is soft-deleted through cascade behavior, related cars/messages are hidden by query filters, and active refresh tokens are revoked. Frontend removes user from table.
If actor is not authenticated, request is unauthorized. If actor is not Admin, request is forbidden. If user does not exist, request returns not found.
Deleted user no longer appears in normal queries. Related listings/messages are hidden by soft-delete filters. User sessions are revoked.
JWT authentication shall validate issuer, audience, lifetime, signing key, and security stamp. Configured access-token expiry is 60 minutes, with 7-day refresh-token lifetime.
Refresh tokens shall be stored as SHA-256 hashes, rotated on refresh, revoked on logout/password reset/password change/user deletion, and protected against reuse detection.
Passwords shall be hashed using bcrypt and must satisfy the backend password policy: minimum 12 characters with uppercase, lowercase, number, and special character.
Login shall lock an account for 15 minutes after 5 failed login attempts.
Role-based authorization shall restrict Admin endpoints to
users and listing updates/deletes to owner or Admin.
Refresh-token cookies shall be HTTP-only, scoped to
, SameSite Lax by configuration, and Secure outside development or when SameSite=None.
Car listings shall be paginated with default page size 10 and maximum page size 50.
Database indexes shall support common queries on users, car owner, brand, creation time, message sender/receiver, delivery, and seen status.
AI price estimation shall call the ML service with a 2-second timeout; if it fails, the backend shall return a fallback estimate.
SignalR chat events should reach connected clients in near real time, preferably under 1 second in normal local/network conditions
The backend shall remain separated into API, Application, Domain, and Infrastructure layers to support future growth.
SignalR currently uses an in-memory connection tracker suitable for one API instance; multi-instance deployment would require a distributed backplane or shared presence store
API errors shall be returned as RFC 7807
Real-time notification failure shall not cancel successful message persistence.
; the backend shall remain usable through fallback pricing when ML prediction is unavailable.
The frontend shall provide marketplace search/filtering, pagination, loading states, empty states, dashboard cards, chat UI, and AI suggested price application.
Validation shall be centralized using FluentValidation, while controllers delegate business logic to application services.
Unit/integration tests shall cover validators, auth security, refresh-token flow, authorization, messages, AI price estimate, dashboard, and car pagination.
Password hashes, refresh-token hashes, and reset-token hashes shall never be returned in API DTOs.
Database consistency shall be maintained through EF Core relationships, migrations on startup, unit-of-work saves, and query filters for soft-deleted users/cars/messages.
The system uses the following data sources:
Seller submits listing form or multipart form with images
Egyptian listing websites, dealer price sheets, auction/import data, verified sale outcomes
The current ML dataset detected in the project contains 12,000 records plus a header in
. The ML service defaults to generating 12,000 records and enforces a minimum synthetic dataset size of 10,000 rows. A legacy
file contains 48 records plus a header and should not be considered sufficient for final model training.
Audi, BMW, Chevrolet, Honda, Hyundai, Kia, Mercedes, MG, Nissan, Skoda, Toyota
35 models, including Corolla, Sunny, Sportage, Tucson, Octavia, X3, C180, MG 5, and others
Petrol, Diesel, Hybrid in ML dataset; backend enum also supports Gasoline, Electric, PlugInHybrid, and CNG
Manual and Automatic in ML dataset; backend enum also supports CVT and SemiAutomatic
Cairo, Giza, Alexandria, Dakahlia, Sharqia, Gharbia, Qalyubia, Monufia, Ismailia, Suez, Assiut, Luxor, Aswan
new, used, very_used in synthetic dataset

Noise tolerance: the synthetic generator adds approximately +/-5% random price noise to simulate marketplace variation. The ML pipeline should tolerate moderate real-world noise caused by negotiation, vehicle condition differences, and listing inaccuracies.

Outlier handling: the training pipeline removes invalid rows, clips mileage to 0-300,000, clips price to 50,000-12,000,000, rejects invalid years, and normalizes categorical fields. The .NET AI response additionally clamps final Egyptian-market estimates to 80,000-6,000,000.
is computed at inference time and is not stored as a dataset column. For future labelled evaluation data
, the low/normal/high classes should be balanced so that no class falls below 20% of the labelled sample, with an ideal distribution near one-third per class.
. Input features include brand, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission, condition, and derived features such as car age, mileage per year, condition score, and brand tier.
as an estimated market range around the estimated price. In the current code, classification is based on the point estimate, while the range is used for market context.

Valid car records must contain non-empty brand and model, valid year, non-negative mileage, positive price, valid fuel/transmission values, location, description, owner ID, and timestamps. Uploaded images must be jpg/jpeg/png and respect backend size limits. Missing or invalid API inputs shall be rejected by validation. Missing ML categorical values shall be normalized to default categories where supported by the ML pipeline.

Fuel and transmission names shall be normalized across API and ML service aliases, for example gasoline/petrol to Petrol/Gasoline and CVT/SemiAutomatic to supported ML transmission categories. Brand and model names shall be trimmed and consistently cased before storage or training.

Synthetic data generation assumes weighted Egyptian-market brand popularity, model base prices, location multipliers, age depreciation, mileage depreciation, condition multipliers, fuel/transmission effects, and random noise. These assumptions are useful for graduation-stage modelling but should be replaced or recalibrated with verified real-market data when available.

The AI-Enhanced Car Marketplace Platform is designed as a modular web application that separates presentation, business logic, persistence, real-time communication, and machine-learning prediction. The frontend is implemented using Next.js, while the backend is implemented using ASP.NET Core Web API following Clean Architecture principles. SQL Server with Entity Framework Core is used for relational persistence, SignalR is used for real-time chat, and a separate Python Flask service is used for machine-learning-based car price estimation.

The system follows two main request paths. The first path handles normal marketplace operations such as authentication, browsing cars, creating listings, uploading images, messaging, dashboards, and admin management:
Next.js Frontend -> ASP.NET Core REST API / SignalR Hub
-> Application Services -> Repositories / Unit of Work
-> SQL Server and wwwroot Upload Storage
The second path handles AI price estimation:
Next.js Listing Form -> ASP.NET Core AIController
-> Application AIService -> Python Flask ML Service
-> Persisted ML Model -> Estimated Price Response

This separation allows the marketplace backend to remain independent from the internal implementation of the ML model. The frontend only receives a structured response containing estimated price, minimum price, maximum price, confidence score, price status, percentage difference, and insights. It does not need to know how the model is trained or served.

The ASP.NET Core backend contains the main API controllers, authentication configuration, SignalR hub, middleware, application services, validators, repository interfaces, and infrastructure implementations. SQL Server stores users, cars, car images, messages, refresh tokens, and password reset tokens. Uploaded files are stored under
. The SMTP email service is used for password reset emails.

The backend is organized around Clean Architecture. Controllers receive HTTP requests and delegate business operations to application services. Services implement use cases such as registration, listing creation, dashboard aggregation, messaging, and AI price estimation. Repositories isolate database access through EF Core. The Unit of Work coordinates persistence through shared
Transfer safe data between API and frontend, such as
Use FluentValidation to validate registration, login, car creation, password reset/change, profile updates, and messages.
Represent persisted business objects such as
Coordinates saving changes to SQL Server using one EF Core context.
manages authenticated real-time connections, typing events, sending messages, presence, and online/offline tracking.
calls the Python ML service and provides fallback estimation if the ML service is unavailable.
Validate and save car images and profile avatars under
Sends password reset emails; development configuration may log fallback reset links.
The frontend is implemented using Next.js and provides separate user flows for authentication, marketplace browsing, listing management, messaging, dashboard, profile, and administration.
Login, register, forgot password, reset password, and logout integration.
Browse cars, search, filter by brand/model, filter by year/price, view details, and paginate results.
Add and edit car listings; create listing form calls AI price estimation while the seller enters data.
Conversation sidebar, chat window, message bubbles, typing indicator, delivered/seen status, and presence display.
Shows total owned listings, total views, unread messages, and recent activity.
Update full name, email, avatar, and password.
View users/listings, update user role, delete users, and delete listings.

Figure 4.3 illustrates the sequence of interactions involved in the AI-based vehicle price estimation process. The seller enters vehicle attributes through the Next.js interface, the backend validates the request and invokes the AI service, which communicates with the Python Flask ML service to obtain a price prediction. In case of ML service failure, a rule-based fallback mechanism is used to ensure system availability.

The AI module is designed as a decoupled pipeline. The Python ML service is independent from the ASP.NET Core backend and the Next.js frontend. The frontend sends car attributes to the backend only. The ASP.NET backend validates the request and calls the Python ML service through HTTP. Therefore, the UI does not depend on Python libraries, model files, or training logic. This satisfies modularity because the ML model can be retrained, replaced, or deployed separately without changing frontend screens.
acts as an adapter between the marketplace and the ML service. It calls the Python

endpoint with a short timeout and returns a marketplace-oriented response. If the ML service fails, times out, or returns an invalid result, the backend uses a fallback rule-based estimator. This design protects user experience because the seller can still receive a price estimate even if the ML microservice is temporarily unavailable.
The current ML service uses a synthetic Egypt-market CSV dataset stored as:

The dataset contains 12,000 rows and includes features such as brand, model, year, mileage, fuel type, transmission, condition, location, and price. The service can auto-generate this dataset using the dataset generator if it is missing. It also supports a configurable dataset path through environment variables.
Converting year, mileage, and price to numeric values.
Removing rows with invalid or missing numeric values.
Rejecting invalid years and negative mileage.
Normalizing fuel type, transmission, and condition categories.
Computing a dataset hash to detect whether retraining is required.
Saving model metadata, including dataset name, dataset hash, model version, and training timestamp.

Future real-market data sources may include verified listing websites, dealership price sheets, historical sale records, and automotive valuation reports [Inferred]. Such data would require additional cleaning, deduplication, source reliability checks, and privacy review before use.

The ML pipeline uses both raw and derived features. Raw features include year, mileage, brand, fuel type, transmission, and condition. Derived features are created to improve model learning and better represent vehicle value.
Measures usage intensity relative to age.
Groups brands into premium, mid, and budget categories.
Converts condition category into a numeric quality score.

Categorical features are encoded using one-hot encoding. Numerical features are scaled using standard scaling inside the model pipeline. This design is appropriate for tree-based and ensemble regression models because it allows mixed categorical and numerical data to be processed consistently.
Input normalization is also performed between the ASP.NET backend and the Python ML service. The backend supports fuel values such as
, while the ML service maps these into its supported categories such as

The training pipeline uses a supervised regression workflow. The target variable is car price. The dataset is split into training and testing subsets using an 80/20 split with random seed 42. The persisted model artifact currently reports the following metadata:

The code supports XGBoost when the dependency is available; otherwise it falls back to Random Forest. In the current saved artifact, the trained model type is Random Forest. Model persistence is performed using
, and the artifact is stored as:

The dataset hash is stored in the artifact metadata. If the dataset changes, the service can detect that retraining is required. This prevents unnecessary retraining when the dataset has not changed and supports reproducibility during evaluation.
The inference workflow is designed to serve predictions during the seller listing process.
Lower bound of estimated market range.
Upper bound of estimated market range.
Percentage difference between user price and estimated price.
If the Python service fails, returns an error, or exceeds the backend timeout,
uses rule-based estimation. This fallback is important for availability because listing creation should not become unusable due to ML service failure.

The architecture balances prediction accuracy and user experience. More complex models may improve accuracy, but they can increase inference latency and operational complexity. Tree-based models such as Random Forest and XGBoost are suitable because the dataset is structured and tabular, not image-based or text-heavy. Deep learning would likely increase training and deployment complexity without clear benefit for the current dataset size and feature type.

The Python ML service is separated from the ASP.NET Core backend to avoid coupling .NET application deployment with Python ML dependencies. The backend also uses a short ML timeout and fallback logic, which favors responsive user experience. Therefore, the system prioritizes dependable interaction during listing creation while still benefiting from ML prediction when the service is available.
Supports server-side actions, protected pages, dynamic marketplace routes, forms, chat UI, dashboard, and admin panel.
Provides secure, high-performance REST APIs and integrates authentication, SignalR, middleware, and dependency injection.
Separates API, Application, Domain, and Infrastructure concerns, improving maintainability and testability.
Suitable for structured data such as users, cars, images, messages, roles, tokens, and dashboard aggregates.
Provides repository-backed database access, relationships, query filters, indexes, and migrations.
Supports chat events, delivered/seen receipts, typing indicators, online/offline presence, and last seen.
Provides preprocessing, train/test split, metrics, Random Forest, scaling, one-hot encoding, and pipelines.
Supported by the training code when available; appropriate for structured tabular regression.
Strong baseline for tabular data, handles non-linear relationships and feature interactions.
Enables stateless authenticated API requests with identity and role claims.
Allows access-token renewal while supporting rotation, revocation, hashing, and reuse detection.
Centralizes validation for auth, profile, car, password, and message requests.
Securely hashes user passwords with salt and computational cost.
Documents endpoints and supports API exploration during development.
Used for validators, services, authorization, integration flows, and security-related tests.
LocalDB supports development, while SQL Server supports relational production-style persistence.
Sends reset links for forgot-password flow.
Stores uploaded car images and avatars while database stores URLs.

Next.js was selected because the frontend requires dynamic pages for marketplace browsing, authentication pages, protected user routes, admin pages, and interactive client-side components such as chat and listing forms. It also integrates with NextAuth for frontend session handling.

ASP.NET Core was selected for the backend because it provides strong support for dependency injection, authentication middleware, authorization policies, SignalR, controllers, middleware, and structured API development. It is suitable for a secure marketplace backend that handles user accounts, listings, messaging, admin operations, and AI integration.

SQL Server was selected because the platform data is relational. Users own cars, cars have images, users send and receive messages, messages may reference cars, and users have refresh/reset tokens. EF Core supports these relationships, indexes, migrations, and query filters.

SignalR was selected because marketplace communication requires real-time server-to-client updates. Traditional polling would increase latency and unnecessary network traffic. SignalR provides an appropriate abstraction for message events, presence events, and receipt events.

Python was selected for the ML service because the Python ecosystem provides mature ML tools such as scikit-learn, XGBoost, pandas, NumPy, and joblib. Separating the ML service from the ASP.NET backend avoids mixing Python ML dependencies with the main web API deployment.

Tree-based ML models were selected instead of deep learning because the pricing dataset is structured and tabular. Features such as year, mileage, fuel type, transmission, condition, and brand tier are well suited to Random Forest and XGBoost-style models. Deep learning would add complexity and require larger datasets without a clear advantage for the current graduation-project scope.

The final architecture therefore supports modularity, maintainability, and user-facing performance. The AI pipeline is decoupled from the frontend and backend internals, the backend protects the user workflow through timeout and fallback behavior, and the system provides an inference plan that serves predictions directly inside the car listing process.

The machine-learning component of the AI-Enhanced Car Marketplace Platform is implemented as a Python Flask service for used-car price estimation. The main dataset used by the current implementation is
, located in the ML service data directory. It contains 12,000 synthetic Egypt-market used-car records generated specifically for the project domain. The target variable is
, which represents the estimated vehicle market value in Egyptian pounds.
The dataset includes the following raw attributes:
Vehicle manufacturer, such as Toyota, Kia, Hyundai, BMW, or Mercedes.
Vehicle model, such as Corolla, Sportage, Sunny, or C180.
Fuel category, such as Petrol, Diesel, or Hybrid.
Transmission category, such as Manual or Automatic.
Vehicle condition, such as new, used, or very_used/refurbished.
Egyptian governorate or city associated with the listing.
Target variable used for regression training.
, the current persisted training pipeline uses selected model features such as
, and engineered features. The use of
as direct training features is a possible [Future Work] extension, since they are already available in the dataset and listing workflow.

Preprocessing is required because machine-learning models are sensitive to invalid values, missing values, inconsistent categories, and extreme outliers. Without preprocessing, the model may learn incorrect relationships from noisy data, such as negative mileage, invalid manufacturing years, or unrealistic prices. In the project, preprocessing is performed in
before model training and during inference preparation.
Validate that required dataset columns exist.
Remove rows with invalid or missing numeric values.
Reject years outside the valid range.
Clip mileage to a realistic maximum of 300,000.
Clip price to a realistic market range.
Normalize categorical values such as fuel type, transmission, and condition.
Fill supported missing categorical fields with default values.
Compute a dataset hash for reproducibility and retraining detection.
Missing, non-numeric, or outside valid range
Convert to numeric, drop invalid values, restrict to supported year range.
Convert to numeric, reject negative values, clip to 0-300,000.
Convert to numeric, remove invalid values, clip to 50,000-12,000,000 in the ML pipeline.
Normalize aliases such as gasoline/petrol into a consistent category.
Normalize to supported values such as Manual or Automatic.
Map labels such as excellent/good/fair/poor to supported ML condition categories.
Available but not currently selected in the persisted model
Retained in dataset; direct model-level use is [Future Work].
Available and used in synthetic generation
Retained in dataset; direct model-level use is [Future Work].

The preprocessing design protects the model from unstable training behavior and supports consistent inference. The same normalization principles are also applied when the ASP.NET backend sends data to the Python service, ensuring that backend enum values can be mapped to ML-compatible categories.

The car price estimation problem is a supervised regression problem because the goal is to predict a continuous numeric value: vehicle price. Several modelling approaches can be considered, ranging from simple rule-based methods to advanced machine-learning algorithms.

A rule-based baseline is useful because car pricing can be approximated using domain rules such as depreciation by age, mileage penalties, and brand-based price ranges. The ASP.NET backend uses this idea as a fallback when the ML service is unavailable. However, rule-based models cannot automatically learn complex interactions from data.

Linear Regression is a simple statistical baseline. It is interpretable and efficient, but it assumes mostly linear relationships between input features and price. Used-car pricing is not purely linear. For example, mileage may affect old cars differently from new cars, and premium brands may depreciate differently from economy brands.

Decision Trees can model non-linear rules by splitting the dataset based on feature values. However, a single tree may overfit and produce unstable predictions. Random Forest improves this by combining many trees and averaging their outputs, making it more stable and better suited for noisy tabular datasets.

XGBoost is also suitable for structured regression problems and is supported by the project code if the dependency is available. It can provide high predictive accuracy through gradient boosting, but it may require more careful tuning. In the current saved artifact, the persisted model type is Random Forest Regressor.

Deep Learning models were not selected for the current implementation because the dataset is structured and moderate-sized. Deep learning is more suitable when very large datasets or unstructured data such as images, text, or audio are involved. For this project, tree-based models provide a better balance between accuracy, interpretability, implementation effort, and inference latency.
Simple, explainable, fast, useful as fallback
Cannot learn hidden patterns from data
Useful fallback, not ideal as main model
Weak for non-linear depreciation and feature interactions
Handles non-linearity, stable, good for tabular data, captures interactions
Less compact than linear models, less explainable than a single tree
Strong accuracy on tabular data, supports complex interactions
Requires larger datasets, more computation, and more tuning

Therefore, Random Forest was selected as the current persisted model because it is suitable for structured tabular vehicle data, captures non-linear relationships, handles feature interactions, and is more stable than a single Decision Tree. It also offers a practical trade-off between prediction quality and inference latency, which is important because price estimation is called from the seller listing form.

This satisfies KSI 1 because the chosen algorithm is technically justified over simpler baselines: it improves on rule-based and linear approaches by learning non-linear market patterns, while remaining less complex and more appropriate than deep learning for the current dataset.

Feature engineering in this project is based on used-car market domain knowledge. Vehicle prices are affected not only by raw attributes, but also by derived relationships such as age, usage intensity, brand perception, and condition quality.
Older vehicles generally depreciate because of wear, outdated technology, and reduced resale demand. Vehicle age is therefore more directly meaningful than the manufacturing year alone.
This feature captures usage intensity more fairly than raw mileage. For example, 80,000 km on a two-year-old car indicates heavy usage, while the same mileage on a ten-year-old car may be normal.

groups brands into market categories such as premium, mid-range, and economy/budget. This reflects market perception and expected resale value. For example, BMW and Mercedes are treated differently from Chevrolet or Hyundai because brand reputation affects price expectations.
converts the categorical condition into a numeric quality signal. In the current pipeline, condition labels such as
are mapped to numeric scores. This allows the model to learn the effect of vehicle condition on price.
The selected raw features include vehicle attributes that directly affect used-car value.
are used in the current model pipeline. The dataset also includes
; however, these are not direct features in the current persisted model and are suitable [Future Work] additions.

Categorical features are processed using one-hot encoding with unknown-category handling. This allows the model to accept categories that may not have appeared during training without failing. Numerical features are processed through scaling inside the pipeline where applicable.
Captures manufacturer reputation and market segment.
Available in dataset; model-specific demand is important [Future Work for direct model use].
Reflects buyer preference and vehicle class.
Represents quality and expected maintenance state.
Available in dataset; regional demand may affect price [Future Work for direct model use].
Represents depreciation more clearly than year alone.
Captures usage intensity relative to vehicle age.
Converts condition into model-readable quality score.

These feature engineering decisions satisfy KSI 3 because they are based on used-car market knowledge rather than arbitrary experimentation. Age, mileage intensity, brand reputation, and condition are all meaningful factors in vehicle valuation.

The current implementation uses fixed, reasonable hyperparameters to support stable graduation-stage training and reproducibility. The current persisted model artifact is a Random Forest Regressor. The code also supports XGBoost if the dependency is installed.

is not currently implemented, the model can be optimized in future work through a controlled search process. Hyperparameter optimization should use a training/validation split or cross-validation. The test set should not be repeatedly used during tuning, because that would lead to overly optimistic evaluation results.
RMSE, to penalize large price errors.
MAE, to measure average absolute pricing error.
Controls number of trees; more trees may improve stability but increase training cost.
Controls minimum samples required to split a node.
Controls minimum samples required in leaf nodes.
Controls feature sampling and tree diversity.
Controls row sampling to reduce overfitting.
1. Split data into training and validation/test subsets.
3. Train models using cross-validation or validation split.
5. Select the model with the best validation performance.
6. Evaluate the final selected model once on the test set.
7. Persist the selected model artifact with metadata.
This satisfies KSI 2 because the hyperparameter search space is explicitly documented, while also honestly distinguishing current implementation from [Future Work] tuning.
The ML service is implemented in three main Python files:
Generates the synthetic Egypt-market used-car dataset.
Loads data, preprocesses it, engineers features, trains/evaluates the model, and saves the model artifact.
Starts the Flask API and serves prediction, metrics, and health endpoints.

generates 12,000 synthetic used-car records. The generator simulates Egypt-market vehicle pricing by applying brand/model base prices, brand multipliers, depreciation by year, mileage penalty, condition effect, transmission effect, fuel effect, location effect, and random noise.
This synthetic data supports the graduation-project stage by providing a reproducible dataset when real verified market data is not yet available.
implements the core ML pipeline. It performs the following operations:
Applies preprocessing using scaling and one-hot encoding.
Splits data using an 80/20 train/test split with random seed 42.
Trains XGBoost if available, otherwise Random Forest.
Evaluates using MAE, RMSE, and R2.
Stores metadata such as model version, dataset hash, dataset size, feature count, and training timestamp.

exposes the trained model as a Flask API. It loads the dataset, checks whether a trained artifact already exists, and loads or retrains the model depending on metadata and dataset hash. It exposes three endpoints:
Returns MAE, RMSE, R2, model type, and model version.
The Flask service normalizes input received from the ASP.NET backend. For example, backend values such as
are mapped to ML-compatible fuel categories.
The ASP.NET backend integrates with the ML service through
It validates core input fields such as brand, model, year, and mileage. Then it delegates estimation to
