endpoint and enriches the ML output into a frontend-ready response. It calculates:
If the ML service is unavailable or times out,
uses rule-based fallback estimation. This protects the user workflow and prevents the listing form from depending completely on the availability of the Python service.
3. Convert year, mileage, and price to numeric values.
4. Remove rows with missing or invalid numeric values.
5. Reject invalid years and negative mileage.
6. Clip mileage and price to realistic ranges.
8. Engineer car_age, mileage_per_year, brand_tier, and condition_score.
9. Split dataset into training and test sets using 80/20 split and seed 42.
11. Train XGBoost if available; otherwise train Random Forest.
1. Frontend sends car attributes to ASP.NET /api/AI/price-estimate.
4. AIService sends supported attributes to Flask /predict.
7. Flask applies preprocessing and feature engineering.
9. AIService adjusts prediction for marketplace response.
11. If userPrice exists, calculate priceStatus and percentageDifference.
12. If Flask fails or times out, use rule-based fallback.
The ML integration uses the Adapter Pattern. The Python Flask service returns a technical prediction response containing
adapts this output into the application DTO required by the frontend, including price range, confidence score, price classification, percentage difference, and insights.
The ML workflow also follows the Pipeline Pattern:
ETL -> Preprocessing -> Feature Engineering -> Training -> Evaluation -> Model Artifact -> Inference
This design makes the ML system easier to maintain because each stage has a clear responsibility. It also supports future improvements, such as adding real-market data, including
as direct training features, using hyperparameter search, adding SHAP explainability, and implementing scheduled retraining.

This chapter evaluates the machine-learning component of the AI-Enhanced Car Marketplace Platform. The ML task is used-car price estimation using structured tabular vehicle data. The evaluation was executed using the actual project dataset, preprocessing code, model configuration, and evaluation script.
The dataset used in the experiments is
, which contains 12,000 synthetic Egypt-market used-car records. The target variable is

. The dataset was divided into approximately 9,600 training records and 2,400 testing records using an 80/20 hold-out strategy. The same split was reused across all experiments to ensure a fair comparison between baseline models, ablation configurations, and statistical significance tests.
Test-set evaluation on the same split for all models
Rule-Based Estimator, Linear Regression, Decision Tree Regressor, Random Forest Regressor
Contains baseline comparison results for all evaluated models.
Contains raw-features versus engineered-features results and improvement percentages.
Contains paired t-test and Wilcoxon signed-rank test results, plus stored prediction errors.
Bar chart comparing MAE, RMSE, and R² across evaluated models.
Chart comparing raw features against engineered features.
Residual distribution chart for the Random Forest model.
Intel64 Family 6 Model 78 Stepping 3, GenuineIntel; 4 logical processors
The baseline comparison evaluates whether the implemented model performs better than simpler alternatives. Four approaches were evaluated on the same dataset split and preprocessing pipeline.

The Rule-Based Estimator represents the fallback pricing logic. It uses manually defined domain assumptions such as brand baseline, age factor, mileage factor, fuel factor, transmission factor, and condition factor. Linear Regression represents a simple statistical baseline. Decision Tree Regressor represents a non-linear single-tree model. Random Forest Regressor represents the current persisted project model.

Linear Regression achieved the best numerical performance on the current synthetic dataset. It produced the lowest MAE and RMSE and the highest R² value. This result indicates that the synthetic data generator contains relationships that are partially linear after preprocessing and log transformation of the target variable.

The Random Forest Regressor did not outperform Linear Regression in this experiment. However, it significantly improved over the Rule-Based Estimator and Decision Tree Regressor. Random Forest is still retained as the primary project model for architectural and future deployment reasons. It supports non-linear feature interactions, is more flexible for future real marketplace data, and is expected to generalize better when the dataset becomes more diverse and less linearly generated. It can also support future expansion with additional features such as vehicle model, location, market trends, real user listings, and more engineered features.
KSI 1 is satisfied because the proposed ML solution was compared against more than two credible baselines: Rule-Based Estimator, Linear Regression, and Decision Tree Regressor.

Because the ML task is price estimation, it is a regression problem. Therefore, classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score are not appropriate. These metrics require discrete class labels, while the project predicts a continuous numeric price.
The evaluation uses MAE, RMSE, and R².
MAE measures the average absolute difference between the actual price and predicted price. In this project, MAE is directly interpretable in Egyptian pounds.
RMSE penalizes large prediction errors more strongly than MAE. This is important in car pricing because large pricing errors may mislead sellers or buyers.
R² measures how much of the variance in car prices is explained by the model. A higher R² indicates that the model captures more of the pricing pattern in the dataset.
Shows how well the model captures price variation
KSI 3 is satisfied because the evaluation uses regression metrics beyond simple accuracy and correctly avoids classification metrics that do not match the problem type.
An ablation study was conducted to evaluate whether the engineered features improve prediction performance. Two configurations were compared using the same Random Forest configuration.
The engineered features were selected based on used-car market domain knowledge.
captures usage intensity more fairly than raw mileage.
represents market perception of premium, mid-range, and economy brands.
converts vehicle condition into a numeric quality signal.
The engineered feature set improved all three numerical metrics. MAE decreased from 251,067.87 to 249,846.95, RMSE decreased from 350,287.98 to 345,573.30, and R² increased from 0.8109 to 0.8159.

However, the improvements were small. This suggests that the raw features already contain most of the predictive information in the current synthetic dataset. The statistical tests in Section 6.5 also show that the feature engineering improvement was not statistically significant at the p < 0.05 threshold.

KSI 2 is satisfied because the ablation experiment directly measures whether feature engineering improves results. The measured results show a small numerical improvement but not a statistically significant one.

Statistical significance testing was performed using paired absolute errors for each test sample. Since all models were evaluated on the same test set, paired testing is appropriate. Two tests were used:

The tests confirm that Linear Regression significantly outperformed Random Forest on the current synthetic dataset. This agrees with the baseline metrics in Section 6.2. The results also confirm that Random Forest significantly outperformed the Decision Tree Regressor, supporting the value of ensemble learning over a single tree.

For the ablation study, the engineered feature set produced better numerical results, but the improvement was not statistically significant. Therefore, the feature engineering contribution should be described as beneficial but limited for the current dataset.

The experimental evaluation demonstrates that the ML module is functional, measurable, and integrated with the actual project dataset and implementation. The experiments used a consistent 80/20 train-test split,
, and the same test set across baseline comparison, ablation study, and statistical testing.

Linear Regression achieved the strongest numerical performance on the current synthetic dataset, with MAE = 244,486.22, RMSE = 331,447.05, and R² = 0.8307. The current Random Forest Regressor achieved MAE = 249,846.95, RMSE = 345,573.30, and R² = 0.8159. Although Random Forest was not the best numerical model in this experiment, it remains a justified architectural choice because it supports non-linear interactions and is more flexible for future real-world marketplace data.

The ablation study showed that feature engineering improved all metrics, but the improvements were small and not statistically significant. This indicates that the current raw features already explain much of the synthetic dataset’s pricing structure.
